 Low voltage violations
 Increased peak demand

• Component sizing
• Smart charging/discharging
Motivation - I
Data retrieved from Global EV Outlook 2021
Motivation- II
• Focus on the aggregated charging demands
• Neglection of individual vehicle owners’ perspectives
• Cost
• Scarce datasets
• Number of decision variables

PEV Load Modeling
• Developing the individual load models using
Kernel Density Estimation (KDE)
• Random Variables (RVs):
• Arrival time
• Departure time
• Charging amount
Introduction
Characteristics of the data
• Turnstile logs
• The effect of weekend visits is omitted.
• No correlation between arrival and
departure times

Density Estimation of PEV load parameters

Proposed Method:
Bandwidth:
• Grid search with half minute resolution
within an hour

Kernel Function:

and bandwidth

• Individual Load Models
• PEV 3 and 7  narrow
time frame
• Other PEVs  greater
deviations
• Energy requirement
PEV load modeling - III
Coordinated Charging Algorithm
Case 1 : Peak shaving
• Morning peak in charging
demand
• Coordinated charging algorithm
 stretches the morning peak over
a more extended period
 decreases the peak charging
amount
Simulations - I
Case 2: Coordinated charging vs.
First Come First Serve (FCFS)
strategy
• PEV 2 and 6  late arrival, quick
departure
• FCFS scheduling  PEV 6 is not
charged.
• Coordinated charging
 PEV 6 is charged.
 PEV 2 is charged shorter.
Simulations - II
Conclusion
• The use of PDFs based on KDE
• Coordinated charging algorithm
• Complementary effects of individual load models on
charging algorithms
